# COMP382 Homework 4

## Student Responses

## Problem 1: Hashing with Chaining
Let the chain lengths be \(\ell_1,\dots,\ell_m\), where \(\sum_{j=1}^m \ell_j=n\), and let
\(L=\max_j \ell_j\).

Use the following rejection-sampling procedure.

1. Pick a table index \(j\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) uniformly at random.
2. Accept this index with probability \(\ell_j/L\). If rejected, restart at step 1.
3. If accepted, pick an integer \(t\in\{1,\dots,\ell_j\}\) uniformly and return the \(t\)-th key in chain \(j\).

### Correctness (uniform key)
Fix any key \(x\), in chain \(j\). In one trial,
\[
\Pr[\text{trial returns }x]
=\Pr[j]\cdot\Pr[\text{accept }j]\cdot\Pr[x\mid j,\text{accept}]
=\frac1m\cdot\frac{\ell_j}{L}\cdot\frac1{\ell_j}
=\frac1{mL}.
\]
This is the same for every key. Conditioning on success, each key is therefore returned with probability \(1/n\).

### Running time
A trial succeeds with probability
\[
p=\sum_{j=1}^m \frac1m\cdot\frac{\ell_j}{L} = \frac{n}{mL}=\frac{\alpha}{L},
\]
so the expected number of trials is \(1/p=L/\alpha\).

Each failed trial costs \(O(1)\) (choose \(j\), test acceptance). The successful trial additionally selects a random position in a chain and walks to it, costing \(O(L)\) in the worst case.

Hence expected time is
\[
O\!\left(\frac{L}{\alpha}\right)+O(L)=O\!\left(L\left(1+\frac1\alpha\right)\right).
\]

## Problem 2: Longest-Probe Bound for Hashing
Assume open addressing with permutation hashing and \(n\le m/2\).
For insertion \(i\), there are \(i-1\) occupied cells.
Let \(X_i\) be probes used by insertion \(i\).

### (a)
\(X_i>k\) means first \(k\) probes are all occupied. Under permutation hashing,
\[
\Pr[X_i>k]
=\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}\frac{i-1-j}{m-j}
\le\left(\frac{i-1}{m}\right)^k
\le\left(\frac12\right)^k
=2^{-k}.
\]

### (b)
Set \(k=2\log_2 n\). Then by part (a),
\[
\Pr[X_i>2\log_2 n]\le 2^{-2\log_2 n}=\frac1{n^2}=O\!\left(\frac1{n^2}\right).
\]

### (c)
Let \(X=\max_{1\le i\le n}X_i\). Then
\[
\Pr[X>2\log_2 n]
=\Pr\Big[\bigcup_{i=1}^n\{X_i>2\log_2 n\}\Big]
\le\sum_{i=1}^n \Pr[X_i>2\log_2 n]
\le n\cdot O\!\left(\frac1{n^2}\right)
=O\!\left(\frac1n\right).
\]

### (d)
Use tail-sum:
\[
\mathbb E[X]=\sum_{t\ge0}\Pr[X>t].
\]
Split at \(t_0=2\log_2 n\):
\[
\mathbb E[X]
=\sum_{t=0}^{t_0-1}\Pr[X>t]+\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t]
\le t_0+\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t].
\]
For any \(t\), by union bound + part (a):
\[
\Pr[X>t]\le\sum_{i=1}^n\Pr[X_i>t]\le n\cdot2^{-t}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t]
\le n\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}2^{-t}
= n\cdot 2^{-t_0+1}
= n\cdot 2^{-(2\log_2 n)+1}
=\frac{2}{n}
=O(1).
\]
So
\[
\mathbb E[X]\le 2\log_2 n + O(1)=O(\log_2 n).
\]

## Problem 3: Randomized Coloring
Algorithm (polynomial time): independently color each vertex uniformly from \(\{1,2,3\}\).

For any edge \((u,v)\), after choosing \(u\)'s color, vertex \(v\) matches it with probability \(1/3\), so
\[
\Pr[(u,v)\text{ is satisfied}] = 1-\frac13=\frac23.
\]
Let \(Y\) be number of satisfied edges. By linearity of expectation,
\[
\mathbb E[Y]=\sum_{e\in E}\Pr[e\text{ satisfied}]=\frac23|E|.
\]
If \(c^*\) is the maximum possible satisfied-edge count, then \(c^*\le |E|\). Therefore
\[
\mathbb E[Y]=\frac23|E|\ge \frac23 c^*.
\]
So this randomized algorithm achieves expected value at least \((2/3)c^*\) in polynomial time.

## Problem 4: Analysis of Selection Using Recurrence
Let \(T(n)\) be expected comparisons for `SELECT` on size \(n\).
`PARTITION` uses \(n-1\) comparisons.
If pivot ends at rank \(r\), recursive call size is at most \(\max(r-1,n-r)\).
Since \(r\) is uniform in \(\{1,\dots,n\}\):
\[
T(n)\le (n-1)+\frac1n\sum_{r=1}^n T(\max(r-1,n-r)).
\]
By symmetry,
\[
T(n)\le (n-1)+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{s=\lceil n/2\rceil}^{n-1}T(s).
\]
Assume inductively \(T(s)\le cs\) for all \(s<n\). Then
\[
T(n)
\le (n-1)+\frac{2c}{n}\sum_{s=\lceil n/2\rceil}^{n-1}s.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{s=\lceil n/2\rceil}^{n-1}s\le \frac{3n^2}{8},
\]
so
\[
T(n)\le (n-1)+\frac{2c}{n}\cdot\frac{3n^2}{8}
=(n-1)+\frac{3c}{4}n.
\]
To force \(T(n)\le cn\) for all \(n\), it is enough that
\[
(n-1)+\frac{3c}{4}n\le cn
\iff n-1\le \frac{c}{4}n.
\]
This holds for every \(n\ge1\) when \(c\ge4\). With \(c=4\):
\[
T(n)\le (n-1)+3n=4n-1\le4n.
\]
Base case \(T(1)=0\le4\) is true.

Therefore, the smallest integer constant from this recurrence method is
\[
\boxed{c=4},\quad\text{so }T(n)\le4n\text{ for all }n.
\]

## Problem 1: Hashing with Chaining

\[

\[

\[
\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t]

## Problem 3: Randomized Coloring

## Problem 4: Analysis of Selection Using Recurrence

\[

Therefore, the smallest integer constant from this recurrence method is
\[
\boxed{c=4},\quad\text{so }T(n)\le4n\text{ for all }n.

graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP382 Homework4 - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 4/4 problems (100%)
Points: 100/100

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[PASS] Problem 1: Hashing with Chaining (20/20 pts)
       This is a correct and complete solution, equivalent in quality to the reference method. Your probability argument is clean and rigorous, and your expected-time derivation correctly separates failed trials from the successful trial traversal cost. The final asymptotic bound is exactly what was required.

[PASS] Problem 2: Longest-Probe Bound for Hashing (40/40 pts)
       Excellent work throughout all four parts. Your derivations are precise and in some places stronger than the reference (especially part (d), where your tail-sum argument is fully rigorous and clean). You handled dependence concerns appropriately by using upper bounds and union bounds, and all asymptotic conclusions are correct.

[PASS] Problem 3: Randomized Coloring (15/15 pts)
       This is fully correct and matches the expected randomized approximation argument. The probability calculation and expectation argument are both concise and rigorous, and the comparison to c* is exactly the needed finishing step.

[PASS] Problem 4: Analysis of Selection Using Recurrence (25/25 pts)
       Your solution is correct and consistent with the reference target constant. The recurrence is slightly different in form from the reference but is a valid upper-bound approach and leads to the same final result. Algebraic steps are clear and justified, and the minimal integer constant conclusion c = 4 is correct.

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
Outstanding submission. All four problems are mathematically correct, complete, and well justified. You consistently used appropriate probabilistic tools (indicator variables, union bounds, tail bounds, tail-sum expectation) and provided clear asymptotic reasoning. Your Problem 4 analysis used a valid alternative recurrence upper bound and still reached the correct tight integer constant from this method.

============================================================

+\le\left(\frac12\right)^k

+Let \(X=\max_{1\le i\le n}X_i\). Then

+\Pr[X>2\log_2 n]
+=\Pr\Big[\bigcup_{i=1}^n\{X_i>2\log_2 n\}\Big]
+\le\sum_{i=1}^n \Pr[X_i>2\log_2 n]
+\le n\cdot O\!\left(\frac1{n^2}\right)
+=O\!\left(\frac1n\right).

+Use tail-sum:
+\[
+\mathbb E[X]=\sum_{t\ge0}\Pr[X>t].

+Split at \(t_0=2\log_2 n\):
+\[
+\mathbb E[X]
+=\sum_{t=0}^{t_0-1}\Pr[X>t]+\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t]
+\le t_0+\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t].

+For any \(t\), by union bound + part (a):
+\[
+\Pr[X>t]\le\sum_{i=1}^n\Pr[X_i>t]\le n\cdot2^{-t}.
+\]
+Thus
+\[
+\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}\Pr[X>t]
+\le n\sum_{t=t_0}^{\infty}2^{-t}
+= n\cdot 2^{-t_0+1}
+= n\cdot 2^{-(2\log_2 n)+1}
+=\frac{2}{n}
+=O(1).
+\]
+So
+\[
+\mathbb E[X]\le 2\log_2 n + O(1)=O(\log_2 n).
+\]
+
+## Problem 3: Randomized Coloring
+Algorithm (polynomial time): independently color each vertex uniformly from \(\{1,2,3\}\).
+
+For any edge \((u,v)\), after choosing \(u\)'s color, vertex \(v\) matches it with probability \(1/3\), so
+\[
+\Pr[(u,v)\text{ is satisfied}] = 1-\frac13=\frac23.
+\]
+Let \(Y\) be number of satisfied edges. By linearity of expectation,
+\[
+\mathbb E[Y]=\sum_{e\in E}\Pr[e\text{ satisfied}]=\frac23|E|.

+If \(c^*\) is the maximum possible satisfied-edge count, then \(c^*\le |E|\). Therefore

+\mathbb E[Y]=\frac23|E|\ge \frac23 c^*.

+So this randomized algorithm achieves expected value at least \((2/3)c^*\) in polynomial time.

+## Problem 4: Analysis of Selection Using Recurrence

+`PARTITION` uses \(n-1\) comparisons.
+If pivot ends at rank \(r\), recursive call size is at most \(\max(r-1,n-r)\).
+Since \(r\) is uniform in \(\{1,\dots,n\}\):

+By symmetry,

+\[
+\sum_{s=\lceil n/2\rceil}^{n-1}s\le \frac{3n^2}{8},
+\]
+so
+\[
+T(n)\le (n-1)+\frac{2c}{n}\cdot\frac{3n^2}{8}
+=(n-1)+\frac{3c}{4}n.

+\[
+(n-1)+\frac{3c}{4}n\le cn
+\iff n-1\le \frac{c}{4}n.
+\]
+This holds for every \(n\ge1\) when \(c\ge4\). With \(c=4\):

+T(n)\le (n-1)+3n=4n-1\le4n.
+\]
+Base case \(T(1)=0\le4\) is true.
+
+Therefore, the smallest integer constant from this recurrence method is
+\[
+\boxed{c=4},\quad\text{so }T(n)\le4n\text{ for all }n.
